annotations.label: Machine learning

anchor.snippet: is a field of study in artificial intelligence concerned with the development and study of statistical algorithms

annotations.note: Machine learning as a field of study is not a mathematical object formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tom Mitchell's definition of learning

anchor.snippet: A computer program is said to learn from experience E with respect to some class of tasks T and performance measure P

annotations.note: Tom Mitchell's informal definition of learning is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generalisation

anchor.snippet: Generalisation in this context is the ability of a learning machine to perform accurately on new, unseen examples

annotations.note: ML generalisation is an informal notion absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Feasibility (polynomial time)

anchor.snippet: a computation is considered feasible if it can be done in polynomial time

annotations.note: Mathlib lacks a complexity-theoretic notion of polynomial-time feasibility.

annotations.label: Supervised learning

anchor.snippet: Supervised learning algorithms build a mathematical model of a set of data that contains both the inputs and the desired outputs

annotations.note: Supervised learning is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Similarity learning

anchor.snippet: Similarity learning is an area of supervised machine learning closely related to regression and classification

annotations.note: Similarity learning is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Unsupervised learning

anchor.snippet: Unsupervised learning algorithms find structures in data that has not been labelled, classified or categorised

annotations.note: Unsupervised learning is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cluster analysis

anchor.snippet: Cluster analysis is the assignment of a set of observations into subsets

annotations.note: Cluster analysis is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Self-supervised learning

anchor.snippet: self-supervised learning involves training a model by generating the supervisory signal from the data itself

annotations.note: Self-supervised learning is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dimensionality reduction

anchor.snippet: Dimensionality reduction is a process of reducing the number of random variables under consideration by obtaining a set of principal variables

annotations.note: Dimensionality reduction is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Manifold hypothesis

anchor.snippet: The manifold hypothesis proposes that high-dimensional data sets lie along low-dimensional manifolds

annotations.note: Although Mathlib has manifolds, the manifold hypothesis about data is not a mathematical theorem and is not formalized.

annotations.label: Semi-supervised learning

anchor.snippet: Semi-supervised learning falls between unsupervised learning

annotations.note: Semi-supervised learning is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Weakly supervised learning

anchor.snippet: In weakly supervised learning , the training labels are noisy, limited, or imprecise

annotations.note: Weakly supervised learning is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reinforcement learning

anchor.snippet: Reinforcement learning is an area of machine learning concerned with how software agents ought to take actions in an environment to maximise some notion of cumulative reward

annotations.note: Reinforcement learning is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Self-learning (CAA)

anchor.snippet: Self-learning, as a machine learning paradigm, was introduced in 1982 along with a neural network capable of self-learning

annotations.note: Self-learning (CAA) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Feature learning

anchor.snippet: Feature learning algorithms, also called representation learning algorithms, often attempt to preserve the information in their input

annotations.note: Feature learning is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sparse dictionary learning

anchor.snippet: Sparse dictionary learning is a feature learning method where a training example is represented as a linear combination of basis functions

annotations.note: Sparse dictionary learning is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sparse dictionary learning is NP-hard

anchor.snippet: The method is strongly NP-hard and difficult to solve approximately

annotations.note: Mathlib lacks NP-hardness machinery, so this complexity result is not formalized.

annotations.label: Anomaly detection

anchor.snippet: anomaly detection, also known as outlier detection, is the identification of rare items, events or observations that raise suspicions by differing significantly from the majority of the data

annotations.note: Anomaly detection is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Robot learning

anchor.snippet: Robot learning is inspired by a multitude of machine learning methods

annotations.note: Robot learning is outside Mathlib's scope.

annotations.label: Association rule learning

anchor.snippet: Association rule learning is a rule-based machine learning method for discovering relationships between variables in large databases

annotations.note: Association rule learning is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rule-based machine learning

anchor.snippet: Rule-based machine learning is a general term for any machine learning method that identifies, learns, or evolves "rules"

annotations.note: Rule-based machine learning is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Supermarket association rule

anchor.snippet: found in the sales data of a supermarket would indicate that if a customer buys onions and potatoes together, they are likely to also buy hamburger meat

annotations.note: An applied retail example outside Mathlib's scope.

annotations.label: Learning classifier systems

anchor.snippet: Learning classifier systems (LCS) are a family of rule-based machine learning algorithms that combine a discovery component

annotations.note: Learning classifier systems are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Inductive logic programming

anchor.snippet: Inductive logic programming (ILP) is an approach to rule learning using logic programming as a uniform representation

annotations.note: Inductive logic programming is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Machine learning model

anchor.snippet: A machine learning model is a type of mathematical model that, once "trained" on a given dataset, can be used to make predictions or classifications on new data

annotations.note: An ML model is not a Mathlib object.

annotations.label: Artificial neural network

anchor.snippet: An ANN is a model based on a collection of connected units or nodes called " artificial neurons "

annotations.note: Artificial neural networks are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Deep learning

anchor.snippet: Deep learning consists of multiple hidden layers in an artificial neural network

annotations.note: Deep learning is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Decision tree learning

anchor.snippet: Decision tree learning uses a decision tree as a predictive model to go from observations about an item

annotations.note: Decision tree learning is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Random forest regression

anchor.snippet: Random forest regression (RFR) falls under the umbrella of decision tree-based models

annotations.note: Random forests are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Support-vector machines

anchor.snippet: Support-vector machines (SVMs), also known as support-vector networks, are a set of related supervised learning methods used for classification and regression

annotations.note: SVMs are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Regression analysis

anchor.snippet: Regression analysis encompasses a large variety of statistical methods to estimate the relationship between input variables and their associated features

annotations.note: Regression analysis as a statistical methodology is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multivariate linear regression

anchor.snippet: Multivariate linear regression extends the concept of linear regression to handle multiple dependent variables simultaneously

annotations.note: Multivariate linear regression is not formalized as a statistical procedure in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bayesian network

anchor.snippet: A Bayesian network, belief network, or directed acyclic graphical model is a probabilistic graphical model that represents a set of random variables and their conditional independence with a directed acyclic graph

annotations.note: Bayesian networks are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gaussian process

anchor.snippet: A Gaussian process is a stochastic process in which every finite collection of the random variables in the process has a multivariate normal distribution

annotations.note: Gaussian processes are defined as `ProbabilityTheory.IsGaussianProcess` in the Gaussian distributions library.

annotations.label: Genetic algorithm

anchor.snippet: A genetic algorithm (GA) is a search algorithm and heuristic technique that mimics the process of natural selection

annotations.note: Genetic algorithms are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Theory of belief functions

anchor.snippet: The theory of belief functions, also referred to as evidence theory or Dempster–Shafer theory, is a general framework for reasoning with uncertainty

annotations.note: Dempster–Shafer belief functions are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rule-based machine learning (RBML)

anchor.snippet: Rule-based machine learning (RBML) is a branch of machine learning that automatically discovers and learns 'rules' from data

annotations.note: Rule-based machine learning is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Federated learning

anchor.snippet: Federated learning is an adapted form of distributed artificial intelligence to train machine learning models that decentralises the training process

annotations.note: Federated learning is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Overfitting

anchor.snippet: Settling on a bad, overly complex theory gerrymandered to fit all the past training data is known as overfitting

annotations.note: Overfitting is an informal statistical-learning notion not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Brown-horse image classifier

anchor.snippet: an image classifier trained only on pictures of brown horses and black cats might conclude that all brown patches are likely to be horses

annotations.note: An ML failure-mode illustration outside Mathlib's scope.

annotations.label: Holdout and K-fold cross-validation

anchor.snippet: Classification of machine learning models can be validated by accuracy estimation techniques like the holdout method

annotations.note: Cross-validation procedures are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Explainable AI

anchor.snippet: Explainable AI (XAI), or Interpretable AI, or Explainable Machine Learning (XML), is artificial intelligence (AI) in which humans can understand the decisions or predictions made by the AI

annotations.note: Explainable AI is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tensor Processing Units

anchor.snippet: Tensor Processing Units (TPUs) are specialised hardware accelerators developed by Google specifically for machine learning workloads

annotations.note: TPUs are hardware, not a mathematical object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Neuromorphic computing

anchor.snippet: Neuromorphic computing refers to a class of computing systems designed to emulate the structure and functionality of biological neural networks

annotations.note: Neuromorphic computing is outside Mathlib's scope.

annotations.label: Physical neural network

anchor.snippet: A physical neural network is a specific type of neuromorphic hardware that relies on electrically adjustable materials

annotations.note: Physical neural networks are hardware concepts not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Embedded machine learning

anchor.snippet: Embedded machine learning is a sub-field of machine learning where models are deployed on embedded systems with limited computing resources

annotations.note: Embedded machine learning is outside Mathlib's scope.
